X2.1.label: Question X2.1
X2.1.scenario: A healthcare insurer is considering introducing a no claims discount (NCD) scheme for its individual PMI contracts. The NCD scale will have discount levels of 0%, 10% and 25%. A claim-free year will increase the discount by one step, unless the maximum discount has already been attained. One or more claims in a year will reduce the discount level to 0%. The policy will indemnify the insured against the total cost of each claim.
X2.1.synopsis: Question regarding the introduction of a no claims discount (NCD) scheme for individual PMI contracts.

(i).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages for the insurer of this NCD scheme.

Advantages and disadvantages for the ins: Claims history will be an indication of expected future claims. Those offered a discount are therefore likely to be better risks, and so are more likely to renew. [½]
Similarly, premium rates for high risks may be greater than the rates with no NCD scheme, and so these risks may be able to get cheaper cover from an insurer without an NCD scheme. [½]
The insurer's overall experience may therefore improve. [½]
Premiums actually paid on each policy will therefore be more closely related to the expected annual claims costs, and so the rating system will be fairer. [½]
It may reduce the number of claims, reducing overall claims costs and cutting claims' administration costs. This is likely to result in more competitive premiums. [½]
It may result in a higher proportion of renewals from year to year, particularly if other insurers do not follow the insurer's lead. [½]
This will help in spreading the initial expenses of selling a policy over the first few years, thus resulting in more competitive premiums. [½]
It will require changes to systems to keep appropriate claim records and update premiums, and so policies will become more costly to administer. [½]

It may allow the company to attract business from other insurers by offering transferring policyholders an immediate discount if they can demonstrate an appropriate no-claims record with their current insurer. [½]
Alternatively, initial underwriting may enable the insurer to determine if the proposer has been healthy for the last year, say, and so to offer an immediate discount of, say 10%. [½]
This may be effective in increasing new business volumes, but the cost of this discount will have to recouped elsewhere (eg by charging higher premiums overall). [½]
The scheme may be seen as unethical, because it could encourage policyholders to delay or not to seek treatment when they may need it. [½]
It will tend to discourage claims for non-essential treatment, which may be acceptable. [½]
If the NCD scheme is novel in the market, this may attract business. [½]

(ii).question:
The insurer's initial calculations suggest that premiums with a 25% NCD will be roughly equal to those for a policy with no NCD scheme, but with an excess of £1,000 on each claim.

Compare these two alternative policy designs.

Comparison with the use of £1,000 excess: Both methods will have the effect of reducing the number of claims, therefore reducing overall costs and hence premiums. [½]
In fact, an excess might be more successful than the NCD scheme in reducing the number of claims. [½]
An NCD scheme gives access to full benefits, whereas an excess scheme will provide reduced benefits. [½]
Both schemes are attractive to those looking for a competitive premium. The NCD scheme will be attractive to all sections of the market, ... [½]
... but the high excess policy will tend to attract the better-off, who have the resources to self-pay for smaller claims. [½]
The better-off may have marginally better health, and so the excess scheme will select slightly better risks. [½]
Whereas the NCD scheme will attract those with good claims experience, which are likely to be significantly better risks. [½]
The NCD scheme will discourage renewal by worse risks, whereas the excess scheme will not. [½]
The excess scheme is likely to raise fewer ethical objections. [½]

This is because the policyholder decided at the date of policy issue that they wished to pay part of each claim, rather than being faced with the decision to pay a higher premium following the occurrence of an insured event. [½]
The excess scheme is less likely (in most markets) to be a novel policy feature, and so will not attract as much attention as the NCD scheme. [½]
The excess scheme may therefore provide fewer opportunities for attracting business from other insurers. [½]
The excess scheme will be easier than the NCD scheme to administer and implement. [½]

(iii).question:
Following a successful introduction of the NCD scheme on its individual PMI business, the insurer is considering extending the idea to its individual income protection and short-term accident and sickness insurance policies.

Discuss whether or not this is a feasible idea for any or all of this business.

NCD scheme for income protection (IP) an: Policyholders usually expect a level guaranteed premium rate on long-term policies, such as IP. [½]
In addition, they are usually underwritten in some detail at the outset and so there are fewer advantages to be gained from subsequent experience rating. [½]
Consequently, standard NCD schemes are rarely used for long-term business. [½]
For benefits in the form of income (such as IP and accident and sickness), the definition of a claim, and therefore, what constitutes a claim-free year is not clear cut. [½]
For example a sickness claim lasting more than one policy year could be considered as one claim or two claims. [½]
IP policies with reviewable premiums are a form of NCD for long-term business, but here the 'discounts' are determined on a portfolio basis rather than on an individual policyholder basis. [½]
Also, discounts often relate to overall experience not just the claims experience (and can also be negative). [½]

The deferred period on an IP policy has the effect of reducing the number of claims, and so any NCD type scheme is less likely to provide additional reductions in the number of claims as a result of deterring people from claiming. [½]
Claims on IP policies, particularly those with long deferred periods, are relatively rare. An NCD scheme would therefore have less impact than for a PMI policy, where claims are more frequent. [½]
An NCD scheme may prove attractive to customers requiring comprehensive cover but at a 'discounted' price. [½]

For example, self-employed professionals, such as dentists, tend to buy policies with very short deferred periods, which can be expensive, but they may see the NCD scheme as a way of reducing their costs without reducing potential benefits. [½]
An NCD scheme would be possible for one-year renewable contracts such as accident and sickness and would have similar advantages to those described above for PMI policies. [½]

X2.2.label: Question X2.2
X2.2.scenario:
A developed country is considering changing its tax regime for long-term insurers. A health and care insurer in the country specialises in income protection (IP) insurance.

It has been suggested that insurers move from being taxed on investment income / gain less expenses, to tax on trading profits. The following formula has been proposed for the determination of the taxable profit under the new regime:

P + I' + A' - E - C - (V1 - V0) - L

where:

P = premiums received
I' = share of investment income
A' = share of change in value of the assets - this may be negative
E = expenses including commission
C = benefit payments
V0 = value of liabilities at beginning of year
V1 = value of liabilities at end of year
L = absolute amount of any loss brought forward from previous year end.

A health and care insurer in this country is concerned about the implications of future changes in the taxation of its individual income protection insurance product if the proposed taxation regime is implemented.
X2.2.synopsis: Question on the impacts of a change in taxation regime to trading profits for an income protection insurer.

(i).question: Describe the impacts that a change in taxation regime could have on the insurer's business.

Impact of tax changes on the insurer's b: The taxation regime can impact the amount of tax paid and when it is paid, for both the insurer and the policyholder. [½]
This could lead to capital strain for an insurer if it is required to pay more or earlier tax than previously ... [½]
... and would reduce profitability and return on capital. [½]

The tax regime, and particularly how policyholders are taxed on the benefits of health and care insurance products, can affect the attractiveness of such products and their willingness to purchase cover. [½]
Policyholders could, for example, have to start paying tax on IP insurance benefits, reducing the actual benefit received. [½]
Alternatively, they could have to start paying tax on premiums, or lose the right to claim tax relief on premiums. [½]

The tax regime may offer tax advantages to insurers with the intention that these are passed on to policyholders to try and encourage them to purchase a particular health and care insurance product. [½]

For example, the rate applied to health and care insurers could vary by product, perhaps to encourage take up of products such as IP insurance that may reduce individuals' reliance on State-provided benefits. [½]

How the taxation regime is structured can affect the design of health and care insurance products, eg IP insurance products could have a minimum term of 5 years in order to receive tax-free benefits. [½]

If different types of providers are subject to different taxation regimes, it could be more (or less) advantageous for health and care insurers compared to other providers, making their products more (or less) attractive to customers compared to others. [½]
This is perhaps less likely for IP insurance than products such as long-term care insurance where there is a greater savings element. [½]
However, banks may be able to offer a product that is similar to IP insurance if a different tax treatment was in place. [½]
There may be changes to the way that different asset types are taxed that make one type more attractive than another, for example the balance between tax on income and gains might change. [½]
The government may choose to apply taxation changes to future new business only, which affects the sustainability of the contract. [½]
If the insurer increases premiums to offset an increase in a tax on its profits, this may reduce new business sales. [½]
However, a change in how IP insurance benefits are taxed in the hands of policyholders could affect both existing and new business volumes. [½]

(ii).question:
Describe the possible impact on the insurer's taxation (once the suggested change is implemented) of the following changes:

(a) Giving all existing policyholders a 1% increase on their sum assured. [2]
(b) Introducing a product variant with the same expected overall profit but with premiums that increase over the duration of the policy. [2]
(c) Changes in the asset-liability matching strategy that could involve higher coupon gilts being sold and lower coupon gilts being bought. [3]
(d) Accelerating the claims processes, so that claims are settled on average three months sooner. [1]

(a) 1% increase to sum assured: There will be a corresponding one-off increase to reserves. [½]
This will make the item V1 and hence (V1 - V0) immediately bigger than it would otherwise have been. [½]
Since this is a deduction from tax, it would reduce the tax paid in the current year. [½]
... but so too will be the future releases from reserves which will largely offset this. [½]

The impact in future years will therefore only be second order, in respect of the release of any prudential margin on the value of the additional sum assured, creating a small profit that will incur a small additional tax liability. [½]
Any implementation costs will reduce tax in the first year. [½]
(b) Increasing premiums: Tax increases with premiums received, so this would tend to defer the tax payments. [½]
However, delaying the receipt of premiums would also tend to reduce reserves. [½]
This will increase profits at the start of the policy and hence accelerate tax payable, offsetting the premium effect. [½]
Overall, the impact may be a deferral of profit - particularly if development expenses are incurred up front. [½]
But since the expected profit is the same, the total tax payable over the lifetime of the product would also be the same - provided tax rates remain unchanged. [½]
(c) Move to lower coupon gilts: The investment income part of the taxation calculation will be reduced, so this would reduce the tax payable. [½]
However, this will be offset by a rise in either the final redemption payment or the value of the bond over time. [½]
If the gross redemption yields are broadly unchanged by the transaction then the total investment return (income plus capital gains) arising each year should also be unchanged. [½]

Hence there would be no material impact on the taxation, although since a greater proportion of the contribution from these assets is in the form of mark-to-market value change rather than fixed income, the amount of tax paid in each year may be more volatile. [½]
If the gross redemption yields are slightly higher, then investment return accruing each year (and hence taxation) would increase - and vice versa if lower. [½]
However, the difference in yields is unlikely to be significant unless the outstanding terms have also changed (and depending on the shape of the yield curve). [½]
If any gains are crystallised, this may cause there to be capital gains tax payable or if any losses are crystallised then these may be available to offset gains elsewhere. [½]
There may be a secondary impact on the timing of taxation if the reserves change due to any difference in gross redemption yields on the high and low coupon bonds. [½]
(d) Accelerating claims settlements: Some claims will move forward to the financial year prior to when they would otherwise have been paid. [½]
This should be broadly offset by a similar release of reserves as the outstanding claims reserve reduces. [½]
Settling claims earlier will decelerate the tax due but will not change the total amount (unless tax rates were changing between financial years). [½]
The acceleration of the process might mean that claims expenses are higher, which would reduce profit and thus tax. [½]

There also may be a second order offsetting impact due to reserves which might be slightly increased due to less discounting of claims - but only if the settlement delay was previously being allowed for explicitly. [½]

X2.3.label: Question X2.3
X2.3.scenario:
The government of a developed country that provides long-term care free for all citizens is facing a crisis in long-term care provision over the next 20 years.

As a result of its concerns, the government is considering the extent of the long-term care that it should provide using public finance.

The government has decided to encourage all its citizens to buy long-term care insurance to cover the need for care after the State's normal retirement age of 65. This insurance will be provided by insurers, but regulated by the government. Care needs before age 65 will be provided through the State's social security system.

Insurance policies satisfying the regulations will be termed 'qualifying policies'. These policies must provide long-term care in a State-approved nursing home for the first three years of incapacity. Any individual who has a qualifying policy will have their continuing care funded by the State after three years, provided in exactly the same way that it was provided during the insured period.

All individuals who need care, whether or not they choose to buy insurance, will be entitled to a non-means-tested benefit related to the number of hours care they are assessed to need each week. The hourly rate will be equal to the minimum wage rate prescribed in the State's labour laws.

The State will regulate both the quality and the maximum price of care in approved nursing homes. These prices will be reviewed annually.

Qualifying policies must:
• have guaranteed level regular premiums
• only use age at entry and gender as rating factors
• have premiums that cease at age 65 or on death, whichever event occurs first
• not acquire a surrender value at any time.

The policies will be available to anyone aged over 18. All funds must be invested in fixed-interest and index-linked government bonds.
X2.3.synopsis: Question on the crisis in long-term care and the design, pricing, and marketing of a qualifying policy.

(i).question: Suggest why this 'crisis in care' may be expected to arise.

The crisis in long-term care: The crisis could be a result of the following factors:
an increased need for care in old age [½]
an increase in the costs of care [½]
insufficient advanced provision for private long-term care. [½]
The increased need for care in old age: The total need for care may be expected to increase rapidly in the future because the sizes of the cohorts that will need care in the next thirty years are much larger than the cohorts receiving care now. [½]
The relative size of these cohorts is determined by:
past birth rates (eg due to baby booms in the late 1940s) [½]
the improving mortality at all ages in successive cohorts. [½]
The need for care is increased by medical treatments that prolong life expectancy whilst in need of care, ie spending a longer period of life with a disability. [½]

However, in the future we may expect that some chronic conditions will be curable or controllable to a greater extent (eg development of treatments for severe arthritis and Alzheimer's disease), and so not require long-term care. [½]
The reduced supply of care: Changing social trends mean that the supply of informal care is likely to fall in the future. [½]
For example, less formal marriages and higher divorce rates, and more geographically dispersed families. [½]
The cohorts making up the working population are smaller than the preceding cohorts due to populations ageing (as outlined above). [½]
So there may be pressures on the supply of labour, meaning the supply of formal care is likely to fall. [½]
The increased costs of care: Increased demand for care, combined with a reduced supply of care workers and care accommodation will lead to an increase in the cost of care. [1]
In addition, the wages of care workers and the cost of suitable accommodation may be expected to rise, at a rate greater than general wage inflation. [½]
The proportion of the population in employment and contributing to tax revenues may be expected to fall. [½]
Insufficient advanced provision: not enough people purchasing insurance to cover LTC costs, eg due to uncertainty over what the State will provide, or [½]
people having little incentive to save for future LTC costs, eg due to State provision being means tested. [½]
This means that most people will need to rely on the State for their long-term care needs. [½]
The consequence of the above factors is that it is expected that the State will not be able to afford to pay for all the care that may be needed. [½]

(ii).question: Describe a possible design for a qualifying policy, including the underwriting considerations.

Terms and conditions: The policy must provide indemnity benefits. [½]
The benefits are equal to the regulated cost of care, against which the insurer can offset the insured's universal care income provided by the State. [½]
The qualifying event is admission to a nursing home. [½]
The insurer therefore will be unable to impose an alternative qualifying test for benefits - for example one based on activities of daily living. [½]
Underwriting: The proposal form can be very simple because the insurer is only allowed to use age at entry and gender as rating factors. [½]
However, the insurer may want to identify lives that present an increased risk, in order to prevent selection against the office. [½]
While the insurer is not permitted to charge extra premiums to lives of the same age at entry and gender, it may be able to refuse cover to lives judged to be a higher risk. [½]

Some conditions resulting in the need for long-term care have a hereditary element, and so questions about family history could be included on the proposal form (or supplementary questionnaires), with the aim of excluding lives that presented an unacceptably high risk. [1]
The insured events for some lives may be a long time (eg more than 40 years) away. [½]

So criteria based on the proposer's current state of health (eg not consulted a doctor or received hospital treatment in the last twelve months) are unlikely to be useful for underwriting, and questions relating to this are likely to be of limited use. [½]

The insurer could use information about risk factors that are not used for rating to identify factors that influence the level of claims (eg occupation or smoker status). This would enable the insurer to refine its marketing strategies so as to attract better risks. [½]
The insurer might want to be more stringent in underwriting proposals from those purchasing cover at advanced ages, say over 55. [½]

(iii).question: Explain the assumptions that might be used in pricing a qualifying policy.

Pricing assumptions: For ages 65 and over, we will need incidence rates for the event of requiring care in a nursing home. [½]
Incidence rates for care will be influenced by medical and social changes. Advances in medical technology may change the range of conditions that require nursing home care. [½]
Social changes may also influence these rates (eg a change in the family circumstances in which State officials decide care is needed). [½]
These rates will be difficult for the insurer to estimate currently, and more so to predict for the future. [½]
Some guidance may be obtained from reinsurers or consultants, who have experience of similar systems in other states. [½]
The policy is likely to be sold with little underwriting, and so general population rather than assured lives mortality will be appropriate in most circumstances. [½]
However, there may be selection against the office (eg those in worse health particularly at older ages choosing to insure), and so some adjustments may need to be made. [½]
The length of a claim is easier to model. The maximum length is three years. [½]
It would be conservative to assume that all those who need care continue to need care until their death (recovery rates are zero). [½]

We will need mortality rates by age for those receiving nursing home care. It may be satisfactory to assume that these rates depend only on age attained, and not also on the time for which the insured has been receiving nursing home care. [½]
Medical technology may lower the rate of mortality of those in care in the future with consequent increases in claims costs. [½]
So it will be necessary to project the mortality rates of those receiving care. [½]

If mortality improves over time, then the expected claims costs will rise because more lives will survive to ages at which they may need long-term care. So it will be necessary to use projected mortality rates for those not receiving care. [½]

The cost of care (nursing home fees per month) is likely to increase at rates above the general rate of wage inflation, because attractive wages will need to be paid to recruit care workers from a shrinking working population. [½]
There may be some increased efficiencies in providing care that will offset these increases, but these are likely to be limited in what is a 'people business'. [½]
The State benefits that can be offset against the claim costs may not increase as fast as care costs, and so the net claim cost will increase more rapidly than wage inflation. [½]
Initial expenses should be relatively low because the product is standardised and the rating factors are simple. [½]
Renewal and policy servicing cost assumptions should be inflated to match expected wage inflation. [½]
Insurers are required to invest funds in government bonds. The expected future return on long-term government bonds is therefore an appropriate rate to use in the pricing basis. [½]
Allowance should be made in the pricing basis for the extent and price of reinsurance. [½]
[Maximum 7]

(iv).question: Suggest how the policy might be marketed and administered.

Sales and distribution: The scheme is 'sponsored' by the government, so the generic product will be widely advertised. [½]
Insurers, as a group, could encourage the government to include a list of the insurers selling qualifying policies as part of this publicity. [½]

Marketing literature could contrast the policy benefits with the minimal alternative support provided by the State, and also mention the additional benefits provided by the State to those who purchase insurance. [½]
It will be important to market this policy to those who are judged to be better risks, eg by advertising in magazines read by well-off consumers. [½]
All qualifying policies have the same terms and conditions - the only difference between policies will be in the premium quoted and the service provided (eg in paying claims). [½]
This means that business volumes should be very sensitive to price and to the standard of service provided. [½]
Keeping acquisition and other expenses low will be important in keeping premiums low. [½]

The permitted rating factors are simple, and so it will be possible to sell the policy through mailshots, through press advertising, over the internet and in other ways that do not require face-to-face contact with the proposer. [½]
However, this may limit the underwriting that can be undertaken. [½]
Because the terms and conditions are prescribed, there is scope for simplifying the supporting policy documentation with consequent cost savings. [½]
The policies do not acquire a surrender value, and so this will encourage persistency and reduce the need for substantial renewal commission. This will help keep premium levels down. [½]
Because there is likely to be a lot of interest in the product, there is the potential for worksite and affinity group marketing. [½]
The reduced initial costs of marketing in this way may allow special discounts to be offered (eg first three months of cover free). [½]
Administration: The premiums are level, regular and guaranteed, and so insisting on electronic premium collection is the best option. [½]
This may exclude a small proportion of potential policyholders, particularly the lower paid. [½]
Forging links with employers and collecting premiums by payroll deduction is another possibility. [½]
Premiums cease at age 65 and no benefits are paid until after age 65, probably much later than this. So, there is the danger of losing contact with the policyholder, ... [½]
... and the consequent difficulty of not knowing when a risk has ended because the policyholder has died. [½]
This might be addressed by circulating an annual newsletter about long-term care to all policyholders. [½]
Entry into a free prize draw to all those who confirm their contact details should help ensure that the database of policyholders is up to date. [½]

(v).question:
The opposition party in the State Parliament has proposed two amendments to the scheme:
(a) individuals should be entitled to tax relief on their premiums
(b) policies should have a guaranteed surrender value before age 65 equal to 80% of the premiums paid. There would be no surrender value after age 65.

Discuss the possible impact on an insurer of each of these proposals.

Tax relief on contributions: This will effectively reduce premiums, and so should increase demand. [½]
The increased demand will only come from those with higher salaries (who pay tax), and so the insurer should be prepared to focus extra sales efforts on these markets. [½]
Increased publicity is likely to result from the change, adding a further impetus to demand. [½]
However, it may tempt people to make inappropriate choices, eg buying a long-term care policy instead of the term insurance that they really need. [½]
This could lead to bad publicity and bring the scheme into disrepute, thus reducing the overall level of sales. [½]
It may increase the insurers' administrative costs, depending on how the relief is granted. [½]
The savings from the scheme may encourage insured lives to think about using the 'spare' cash to buy other insurance products. [½]
The government may be tempted to recover some of the cost of tax relief by taxing insurers more in other ways. [½]
Guaranteed surrender values before age 6: This may help to relieve the dissatisfaction of those who cannot afford to continue their premium payments for financial reasons. [½]
Previously these people would have received nothing, perhaps leading to bad publicity, but now they will receive some return. [½]
However, encouraging policyholders to lapse their policies by paying a surrender value will increase the insurer's administrative costs. [½]

However, the penalty of 20% of the premiums paid plus investment income on the premiums paid, could be sufficient to cover all the insurer's expenses including a contribution to the cost of claims. [½]
Each surrender will generate less surplus for the insurer than would be the case without this modification. [½]
But the number of surrenders may increase, and so the total surplus released by surrenders will probably be larger. [½]
However, early surrenders are likely to generate losses for the insurer, and so a significant number of these may result in an overall loss. [½]
In theory, there is an anti-selection risk of surrender by policyholders nearing age 65 and still in good health. [½]

However, this is unlikely because the insured events are so far in the future, that it will be difficult for an individual policyholder to make successful predictions of their need for future care. [½]
More worrying are 'death-bed surrenders'. Those who are very ill and do not expect to survive to receive benefits will surrender immediately they realise this, so as to 'cut their losses'. [½]
While mortality is much lighter before age 65 than after age 65, the effect of this anti-selection could still be significant for the insurer. [½]

X2.4.label: Question X2.4
X2.4.scenario:
A healthcare insurance company, based in a developed country, has a large and profitable portfolio of individual income protection (IP) insurance policies. The majority of this business is sold through financial advisers. The insurer is widely regarded as a market leader in this line of business.

It is compulsory for employers to provide sick pay benefits (at a set level based on the legal minimum wage in the country) for the first 13 weeks of disability. After this, the government of the country provides means-tested benefits but only those on very low incomes will be eligible for any State benefit. Employers can choose to provide enhanced sick pay benefits.

The insurance company is considering launching a group IP product. It would be aimed at companies operating in the manufacturing and construction industries, and would offer group IP insurance for the executives and managers within a company.
X2.4.synopsis: Question on the design, underwriting, claims administration, and marketing of a group income protection product, including the launch of alternative schemes and extensions to family members.

(i).question:
Describe:
(a) the possible terms and conditions of this group IP policy [9]
(b) how the business might be underwritten [4]
(c) how claims might be administered [3]
(d) the factors that should be considered in deciding how the scheme might be marketed. [3]

(a) Policy conditions: The more flexible the benefit structure is, the wider the range of employers to whom the product appeals will be. [½]
The following are examples of the terms and conditions that may be included:
Offer a range of deferred periods, say from 4 to 52 weeks. [½]
Short deferred periods would allow employers to buy insurance to cover the risk of statutory sickness benefits. Longer deferred periods could be offered for lower premiums. [½]
Require early notification of sickness absence (eg halfway through the deferred period). [½]
This will enable the insurer's counselling and advice service to have an early impact and perhaps prevent a claim coming into payment. [½]
An occupation-based definition of incapacity is probably appropriate - for example based on being unable to perform any or all of the duties of their own occupation. [½]
This will match the employees' initial needs. The definition might be changed to a less restrictive one after, say 24 months of incapacity. [½]

Require medical certification of incapacity and re-certification every six or twelve months during periods of incapacity. This is essential if the insurer is to maintain effective control of claims. [½]
Certification should be checked by the insurer and not the employer. [½]

This will allow the insurer to exercise effective claims control, and avoid any temptation that the employer may have if allowing claims would be to the employer's advantage, eg during periods of economic recession. [1]
Enforce a benefit limitation so that there is an incentive for claimant to return to work, for example:
{X% of pre-disability income} - {all sources of continuing income}
where X is between, say, 60 and 75. [1]
This will take account of any ill-health retirement pension that may be paid after claims payments begin. [½]
Pre-disability income will need to take account of bonuses and other irregular payments by averaging over a defined pre-disability period, eg 13 weeks. [½]
Make an allowance for linked claims with a linking period of, say, three to six months. [½]
This will encourage employees to return to work because they know that if they fall sick again benefits will continue without the need to wait until the end of another deferred period. [½]

Allow proportionate benefits on a return to work in a new less well-paid role or rehabilitation benefits on a return to work in a reduced capacity. This will also encourage employees to return to work, and so help to reduce claims costs. [½]
Have a waiting period of employment before employees are eligible to join the scheme, eg twelve months. [½]

This will reduce overall claims, limit anti-selection and help to reduce administrative costs because it will save the costs of arranging for temporary and short-term employees to join the scheme. [½]
Ensure that there is compulsory scheme membership for all eligible employees, or allow voluntary membership but only with a high participation rate. [½]
This will reduce anti-selection. [½]
Offer a stepped benefit structure (eg 100% of salary for the first six months of incapacity, then 50% of salary after six months). [½]
Link benefits in payment to an index of wages, if this is possible, to satisfy the need to maintain prosperity during incapacity. [½]
Link the possible expiry ages for benefits to the range of normal retirement ages in the employer's pension schemes. [½]
[Maximum 9]
(b) Underwriting: Impose a free-cover limit to control morbidity risk. [½]
This may have a significant effect because there is likely to be a wide variation in incomes within the group. [½]
If membership was compulsory or if the proportion of eligible employees joining was high, then the free-cover limit could be more generous than if membership was entirely voluntary. [½]
Those above the free-cover limit would be underwritten, probably using the insurer's individual underwriting procedures. [½]

As a general underwriting criterion, we could insist that all new members must have been continuously at work with no days of sickness absence in, say, the three months immediately prior to joining the scheme. [½]
This is an appropriate criterion as it is directly related to events that lead to claims. [½]
Specific exclusions might be appropriate in cases of chronic disease, eg severe asthma, ... [½]
... but in general we would want to avoid these because the employer will want universal cover and claims administration will be simplified. [½]
The risk factors to be considered in simple underwriting include:
time spent in more dangerous environment (eg supervising on site, on factory floor) [½]
time spent driving as an integral part of the job [½]
time spent overseas as an integral part of the job. [½]
(c) Claims administration: There should be telephone helplines to advise people on their entitlement to State welfare benefits, ... [½]
... on what they can do to manage their condition and on the policy conditions relating to their claim (eg the possibility of part-time working). [½]
Manage claims when they come into payment by using the counselling and advice service to ensure that claimants are aware of all the sources of help available to them. [½]
Rather than doing this directly the insurer could offer training and advice to enable the employer's human resources department to deal with most day-to-day queries. [½]
Require that claimants provide regular evidence of their continuing incapacity, in order to minimise fraudulent claims. [½]
Also, manage the financial aspects of claims by regularly checking on other sources of income that should be offset against the maximum claim entitlement. [½]
Require the employer to make valid claims payments as part of the payroll, and to account to the insurer for these payments on a regular basis, perhaps as an offset against the premium due. [½]
This will help to control claims and reduce the administrative costs of claims for the insurer. [½]
(d) Marketing: Choosing to focus on companies operating in the manufacturing and construction industries may mean that the insurer is exposed to the cyclical nature of these businesses. [½]
Group IP insurance may be seen as a marginal expenditure, and so in an economic downturn some employers may lapse their policies. [½]
The insurer has experience of the financial adviser market. Most financial advisers will specialise in sales of long-term IP policies to individuals. [½]
The group IP market is different as there is no long-term commitment to one insurer. [½]
Employers usually look to specialist brokers to arrange all of their insurance on a competitive basis by regularly re-broking the business to obtain the best rates. [½]
So the insurer will need to establish links to the specialist broker market. [½]
Having a competitive or novel product will help make the product more marketable. [½]
The insurer may need to offer attractive sales remuneration, either in terms of the level of remuneration or the way in which it is paid. [½]
For example, higher initial commissions than the market norm to those brokers who introduce significant amounts of new business. [½]
Higher than normal commissions can be financed by the company in the short term, while it establishes a market share and a reputation in the group IP market. [½]

(ii).question:
The marketing director has suggested that an additional group scheme, offering IP insurance for those employees working on the factory floor or on a construction site, should be launched at the same time.

Comment on how and why this scheme would differ from the scheme for executives and managers.

Scheme for site workers: The question asks for the 'differences', and so issues that are the same as those given for the managers' scheme would not gain credit.
In general, benefits are likely to be lower than for the managers' scheme, ... [½]
... because the employer may feel that recruitment and retention is more important when considering managers. [½]
However, these workers are not as likely as the managers to have comprehensive ill-health retirement benefits. [½]

The employer may be more concerned with providing these workers with benefits related to salary (as opposed to flat rate benefits, for example) than in the case of the management scheme, particularly to cover long-term permanent sickness for these members. [½]
Underwriting: If there is less variation of wage levels between members of a scheme, the free-cover limit will be a less important consideration because it will be less likely to apply. [½]

However, if overtime payments and bonuses are included in the calculation of pre-incapacity income, there is likely to be more variation in incomes between members, and so a free cover limit may have more impact. [½]

Other underwriting procedures can be the same as those for the managers' scheme, except we would want to identify those whose work involved above average risk (eg working with heavy machinery, working at heights, working with hazardous chemicals or materials). [½]
Manual workers will tend to have higher sickness rates. So, all other things being equal, the premium rates will be higher than those for the managers' scheme. [½]
Policy conditions: These workers are likely to be mainly involved in heavy manual work. In these cases, even a slight incapacity (eg a fracture or severe cut) can prevent a person from following their normal occupation. [½]
A less stringent definition of incapacity than inability to follow one's own occupation might be appropriate. [½]
For example, use own occupation for the first 24 months of incapacity and thereafter use inability to follow any occupation to which the insured is suited by education or training. [½]

If there was no prospect of their returning to their old job, employees might welcome the support provided by the employer and the insurer to train for alternative (less physically demanding) work. [½]
Manual workers may have different terms and conditions of employment, particularly those relating to sick pay. So we may need to offer a different choice of deferred periods for the scheme. [½]
Claims administration: Claims will in general be smaller in amount and may be more frequent, ... [½]
... and so will be relatively more expensive to administer. [½]
If the employer deals directly with claimants, the employer may devote fewer resources to these employees than to the more highly paid and vital (from the employer's point of view) managers. [½]

(iii).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages to the insurer of launching both schemes, rather than the single scheme originally suggested.

General: Launching both schemes at the same time exposes the insurer to greater risks. [½]
However, if the products are launched in sequence, the insurer can adapt the second launch using experience gained from the initial launch. [½]
Marketing: Being able to offer a product range that includes all employees will increase the likelihood of securing business. [½]
If an employer only opts for one of the schemes initially, there will be future opportunities for cross-selling. [½]
If we sell both schemes to an employer there will be a greater premium income per sale, and the sales process will be more efficient. [½]
There will be additional costs from launching two schemes, eg marketing literature. [½]
Administration: Business volumes may be greater, making it easier to cover both the costs of development and the fixed costs of administering the new schemes. [½]
Having more business from each employer should make administration more efficient. [½]
For example, the overhead costs of training and liaising with an employer will be a smaller proportion of the premium income. [½]

(iv).question:
The human resources director has suggested that both schemes could be made more attractive to employers by extending cover to each employee's family.

Suggest how the schemes might be extended in this way.

Policy conditions: Not all family members will have a regular occupation, and so having a claim definition that relates to occupation will be difficult. [½]
Using an occupational definition for some family members and an alternative definition for those not in employment would be administratively complex. [½]
It would be possible to devise a personal capability assessment that consisted of a number of tasks related to everyday living, ... [½]
... eg walking a given distance unaided, climbing stairs, ability to understand and follow instructions. [½]
A person would have a valid claim if they were unable to complete a specified number of these tasks. [½]
An alternative would be to use a test related to appropriate activities of daily living. [½]
So for a spouse, we might use tasks such as travelling unaided on public transport, going shopping, ability to deal with money and other financial tasks; ... [½]
... and for children we might use attending school regularly, ability to complete personal tasks independently (eg washing, eating). [½]
It would be possible to offer benefit levels defined in terms of the member's salary (eg 50% of member's benefit for a spouse and 10% of the benefit for a child). [½]
This would have the advantage of relating benefits more closely to the impact of the insured event on the financial position of the family. [½]
The alternative would be a flat rate benefit (eg £10,000 pa for a spouse and £2,000 pa for a child), subject to maximum payment equal to the insured's benefit, say. [½]
Benefits could be linked to an index, probably of national wage levels. [½]

Spouses may have income during incapacity from other sources (eg their own employer's sick pay scheme, statutory benefits) and, although this may be small, it should be deducted from the maximum entitlement. [½]
A long deferred period, eg 26 or 52 weeks, would reduce the cost of claims management and help if there had been limited underwriting. [½]
However, a shorter deferred period may more closely match the risks faced by someone keeping house. But this would increase the costs of this extension of the scheme considerably. [½]
Claims management: The claims criterion is likely to be related to personal capabilities rather than to a medical condition, so the insurer will need to put in place procedures for this assessment. [½]
The assessment would need to be repeated on a regular basis. [½]
If someone seen to be independent of the insurer carried out the assessment procedures, then disputes about assessment should be less likely. [½]
A counselling service could ensure that claimants applied for all the State benefits to which they were entitled and sought remedial therapy (eg physiotherapy to aid recovery). [½]
Underwriting and pricing: If nearly all employees were in the scheme, and cover extended to all their dependants, then the additional premium could be a simple percentage loading based on the main premium. [½]
The loading would be based on an estimate of the average family size of the members of the scheme. [½]

If this were not the case and employees were allowed to decide whether or not they wished to extend cover to their families, there would be considerable opportunity for selection against the insurer, eg those with large families would be more likely to elect to join. [½]
Using additional rating factors (such as family size and age and gender of family members) to determine the additional premiums could reduce this selection. [½]
This would involve considerable data collection because the employer will not have details of the family members. [½]
Medical underwriting of family members could be considered, but this may be costly compared to the savings it achieves. [½]
An alternative is to have a moratorium on claims related to pre-existing conditions, and to require two years free of symptoms and treatment before claims related to these conditions are allowed. [½]

(v).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages to the insurer of extending the schemes in this way.

Marketing: Increased premium income should lead to greater profits. [½]

This feature may make this new policy unique in the market place. This novelty is likely to encourage brokers to actively consider it as an alternative in reviews of providers for their current clients. [½]
The perceived value of the benefit may be much more than the additional premium. This will reinforce its attraction for clients. [½]
Underwriting: Data collection for underwriting would be more complex. [½]

Statistical data to cost the additional risk may be very limited. This may not be a serious problem as the additional benefit is packaged with the main benefit, and some element of cross-subsidy is acceptable. [½]
Administration: Premium collection would be more complex unless the structure was very simple, eg flat % addition to main premium. [½]
The costs of administering these claims may be considerably greater than the costs of employee claims ... [½]

... because while the employer can provide data about the employee (eg demographic details, salary), the employer is unlikely to have data about family members. The insurer will need to acquire this data directly. [½]
Personal capability assessments for dependants will be more costly to administer than the occupationally-based criteria used for employee's entitlement to benefit. [½]
